questionText: A retailer buys goods from a wholesaler at a certain rate. The wholesaler offers 1 dozen free on every purchase of 5 dozens. What is the equivalent discount percentage offered by the wholesaler?

steps.explanation: The retailer gets 1 dozen free on purchase of 5 dozens, so effectively gets 6 dozens by paying for 5.
steps.calculation: Free dozens = 1 out of every 6 dozens received

steps.explanation: Equivalent discount = (free quantity / total quantity) × 100%

solution.shortcut: Equivalent discount = $\frac{n}{n+1} \times 100\%$ where n is free articles and n+1 is total received.
solution.commonMistake: Dividing by 5 (articles paid for) instead of 6 (total articles received).

modelName: Buy-n-get-m-free discount

questionText: By selling 18 cots, a person gains the cost price of 3 cots. If the selling price of 18 cots is Rs. 16800, find the cost price of one cot.

steps.explanation: Gain on 18 cots = C.P. of 3 cots. So S.P. of 18 cots – C.P. of 18 cots = C.P. of 3 cots.
steps.calculation: C.P. of 21 cots = S.P. of 18 cots = Rs. 16800

steps.explanation: Find cost price of one cot.

solution.shortcut: SP of n items = CP of (n + gain_items), so CP per item = SP_total / (n + gain_items).
solution.commonMistake: Dividing 16800 by 18 instead of 21.

modelName: Gain equals CP of some items

questionText: By selling 20 dining tables, a shopkeeper gains the selling price of 4 dining tables. If the selling price of 16 dining tables is Rs. 12000, find the selling price of 1 dining table.

steps.explanation: S.P. of 20 tables – C.P. of 20 tables = S.P. of 4 tables. So C.P. of 20 tables = S.P. of 16 tables = Rs. 12000.
steps.calculation: C.P. of 20 tables = S.P. of 16 tables = Rs. 12000

steps.explanation: Find S.P. of 1 dining table.

solution.shortcut: Gain = SP of 4 means CP of 20 = SP of 16, so SP per table = SP_total / 16.
solution.commonMistake: Dividing by 20 instead of 16.

modelName: Gain equals SP of some items

questionText: A shopkeeper sells 100 pencils and gains the equivalent of the selling price of 20 pencils. What is the gain percentage?

steps.explanation: S.P. of 100 pencils – C.P. of 100 pencils = S.P. of 20 pencils, so C.P. of 100 pencils = S.P. of 80 pencils.
steps.calculation: Let C.P. of each pencil = Re. 1. Then C.P. of 80 = Rs. 80; S.P. of 80 = Rs. 100.

steps.explanation: Calculate gain percentage.

solution.shortcut: Gain% = $\frac{\text{gain items}}{\text{items sold} - \text{gain items}} \times 100\%$
solution.commonMistake: Dividing gain by 100 (total sold) instead of 80 (CP base).

modelName: Gain equals SP of some items

questionText: A fruit vendor buys 36 mangoes and suffers a loss equal to the selling price of 4 mangoes. What is the loss percentage?

steps.explanation: C.P. of 36 mangoes – S.P. of 36 mangoes = S.P. of 4 mangoes. So C.P. of 36 = S.P. of 40 mangoes.
steps.calculation: Let C.P. of each mango = Re. 1. C.P. of 40 = Rs. 40; S.P. of 40 = Rs. 36.

steps.explanation: Calculate loss percentage on 40 mangoes.

solution.shortcut: Loss% = $\frac{\text{loss items}}{\text{bought} + \text{loss items}} \times 100\%$ when CP of bought = SP of (bought + loss).
solution.commonMistake: Dividing loss by 36 instead of 40.

modelName: Loss equals SP of some items

questionText: A person bought 8 apples for Rs. 34 and sold them at 12 for Rs. 57. To earn a profit of Rs. 45, how many apples must he sell?

steps.explanation: Find C.P. and S.P. per apple.
Number of apples needed to earn Rs. 45 profit.

solution.shortcut: Profit per apple = SP per apple – CP per apple; number = target profit / profit per apple.
solution.commonMistake: Not computing per-unit profit correctly from bulk rates.

modelName: Bulk buy/sell – target profit

questionText: Vinod purchased some pencils. By selling at Rs. 2.50 each he gains Rs. 110 more than by selling at Rs. 1.75 each, and the profit at Rs. 2.50 is Rs. 55 more than the loss at Rs. 1.75. How many pencils did Vinod buy?

steps.explanation: Let number of pencils = n. Total gain at Rs. 2.50 minus total receipt at Rs. 1.75 equals Rs. 110 more. Also, profit at Rs. 2.50 + loss at Rs. 1.75 = Rs. 165 (since gain is 110 more and profit at 2.50 is 55 more).
Solve for n.

solution.shortcut: Difference in SP per unit × n = sum of profit and loss.
solution.commonMistake: Setting up the equation with 110 only instead of 110 + 55.

modelName: Two selling prices – number of items

questionText: A person bought some pencils. By selling 8 pencils for Re. 1, he gains Re. 1 on 56 pencils. How many pencils should he sell to gain Rs. 10?

steps.explanation: Re. 1 (profit) is made on 56 pencils. So Rs. 5 is gained on 56 pencils at rate stated (gain of 1 per unit cost × 56).
To gain Rs. 10, number of pencils needed.

solution.shortcut: Scale proportionally: if gain G on N pencils, then for gain G', pencils = $\frac{N \times G'}{G}$.
solution.commonMistake: Not correctly identifying the base gain amount.

modelName: Proportional gain on pencils

questionText: A shopkeeper buys pencils at 6 for Rs. 4 and sells at 4 for Rs. 6. What is the gain percentage?

steps.explanation: Take LCM of 6 and 4 = 12 pencils. C.P. of 12 pencils = $\frac{4}{6} \times 12 = $ Rs. 8.
S.P. of 12 pencils = $\frac{6}{4} \times 12 = $ Rs. 18.
Calculate gain percentage.

solution.shortcut: Take LCM of the two quantities to avoid fractions.
solution.commonMistake: Not using LCM, leading to fractional quantities.

modelName: Buy-m-for-x sell-n-for-y

questionText: A cloth merchant buys 120 cm of cloth for Re. 1 but uses a faulty metre rod of only 80 cm. What is the profit percentage?

steps.explanation: The merchant buys 120 cm for Re. 1, so C.P. of 120 cm = Re. 1, meaning C.P. per cm = $\frac{1}{120}$.
Using a faulty rod of 80 cm, he sells what he measures as 100 cm but is actually 80 cm, charging Re. 1 for 80 cm (80% of Re. 1 per true cm). S.P. of 80 cm = 80% of Re. 1 = $\frac{4}{5}$.
Calculate profit percentage.

solution.shortcut: Faulty measure profit% = $\frac{\text{True length} - \text{Faulty length}}{\text{Faulty length}} \times 100\%$.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing which is the true measure and which is the faulty one.

modelName: Faulty measure profit

questionText: A man buys 6 eggs at Rs. $\frac{1}{2}$ each and 6 eggs at Rs. $\frac{2}{3}$ each, and sells all at 3 for Re. $\frac{3}{5}$. What is his gain percentage?

steps.explanation: C.P. of 12 eggs: 6 eggs at Rs. 1/2 + 6 eggs at Rs. 2/3 = Rs. 3 + Rs. 4 = Rs. 7.
S.P. of 12 eggs at 3 for Rs. 3/5: S.P. = $\frac{3}{5} \times \frac{12}{3} = \frac{3}{5} \times 4 = \frac{12}{5} = 2.40$. Wait — from solution: S.P. = $\frac{3}{5} \times 12 = 7.20$.
Calculate gain percentage.

solution.shortcut: Use equal quantities to find combined CP and SP.
solution.commonMistake: Adding quantities without normalising to a common base.

modelName: Two-rate purchase single-rate sell

questionText: A shopkeeper buys 6 toffees for Re. 1 and sells them at a 20% profit. How many toffees does a customer get for Re. 1?

steps.explanation: C.P. of 6 toffees = Re. 1. S.P. with 20% profit = Rs. $\frac{6}{5}$ for 6 toffees.
For Re. 1, toffees = $\frac{5}{6} \times 6 = 5$.

solution.shortcut: Toffees per Re. 1 = (CP toffees per Re. 1) × $\frac{100}{100 + Profit\%}$.
solution.commonMistake: Applying profit % in wrong direction.

modelName: Buy n for x sell at profit – items per rupee

questionText: A shopkeeper buys 8 pens for Rs. 100 and sells them at a profit of 60%. How many pens does a customer get for Rs. 100?

steps.explanation: C.P. of 8 pens = Rs. 100. S.P. of 8 pens = 160% of Rs. 100 = Rs. 160.
For Rs. 100, pens = $\frac{8 \times 100}{160} = 5$.

solution.shortcut: Pens per Rs. 100 = (bought) × $\frac{100}{100 + Profit\%}$.
solution.commonMistake: Dividing 8 by 1.60 and forgetting to multiply by 100.

modelName: Buy n for x sell at profit – items per rupee

questionText: A man buys toffees at the rate of 12 for Re. 1 and sells them at 10 for Re. 1. To make a profit of Re. 1, how many toffees must he sell?

steps.explanation: Let S.P. of 12 toffees = Rs. x. From ratio: 80 : 1 = 120 : x (where 80 is CP per 120 CP equivalent and 1 is unit), so $x = \frac{120}{80}$.
steps.result: Profit per 12 toffees = Rs. 3/2 – Re. 1 = Rs. 1/2

steps.explanation: To earn profit of Re. 1, sell $12 \times 2 = 24$ toffees? Re-approach: CP rate = 12 for Re. 1, SP rate = 10 for Re. 1. CP per toffee = 1/12, SP per toffee = 1/10. Profit per toffee = 1/10 – 1/12 = 1/60. For profit Re. 1: n = 60.

solution.shortcut: Profit per toffee = SP per toffee – CP per toffee; then n = target profit / profit per toffee.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing rate per dozen vs per unit.

modelName: Buy n for x sell m for y – target profit

questionText: A shopkeeper sold 90 ball pens for Rs. 160 and suffered a loss of 20%. How many ball pens should be sold for Rs. 96 to have a gain of 20%?

steps.explanation: S.P. of 90 ball pens = Rs. 160, Loss = 20%. So C.P. = $\frac{100}{80} \times 160 = $ Rs. 200.
To gain 20%, SP = 120% of CP = $\frac{120}{100} \times \frac{20}{9} = \frac{8}{3}$ per pen.

solution.shortcut: Find CP per pen from loss, then SP per pen at target gain, then divide target revenue by SP per pen.
solution.commonMistake: Using loss% in the wrong formula direction.

modelName: Loss to gain – how many items

questionText: A dealer bought 30 kg of wheat at Rs. 9.50 per kg and 40 kg at Rs. 8.50 per kg. He mixed the two and sold the mixture at Rs. 8.90 per kg. What is his total profit or loss?

solution.shortcut: CP = sum of (qty × rate) for each lot; SP = total qty × selling rate.
solution.commonMistake: Not adding both lots' costs correctly.

modelName: Mixture of two lots – profit/loss

questionText: A shopkeeper bought 25 kg of rice at Rs. 32 per kg and 15 kg at Rs. 36 per kg. He mixed the two and sold the mixture at Rs. 40.20 per kg. What is the profit percentage?

solution.shortcut: Calculate total CP and SP, then find profit%.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong selling price or miscalculating total quantities.

modelName: Mixture of two lots – profit percentage

questionText: A dealer bought 30 kg of wheat at Rs. 11.50 per kg and 20 kg at Rs. 14.25 per kg. He sold the mixture at a 30% profit. What is the selling price per kg? (approximately)

solution.shortcut: Total SP = CP × (1 + profit%/100); SP per kg = total SP / total kg.
solution.commonMistake: Calculating profit on wrong total weight.

modelName: Mixture – SP per kg at given profit%

questionText: A trader mixes 30 kg of rice at Rs. 17.50 per kg with 30 kg of another variety and sells the mixture at Rs. 18.60 per kg, earning a profit of $\frac{1}{5}$ (20%) on the cost. At what price per kg did he buy the second variety?

steps.explanation: Let C.P. of second variety = Rs. x per kg. Total C.P. of 60 kg = Rs. (525 + 30x).
Profit = 20%, so SP = 120% of CP.

solution.shortcut: Set up equation: SP = 1.2 × CP and solve for unknown price.
solution.commonMistake: Setting profit% on SP instead of CP.

modelName: Mixture – find unknown CP from profit%

questionText: A grocer mixes tea worth Rs. 160 per kg with tea worth Rs. 200 per kg in the ratio 5:4. At what price per kg should he sell the mixture to earn a profit of 8%?

steps.explanation: Suppose 5 kg and 4 kg are bought. C.P. of 9 kg = Rs. (5 × 160 + 4 × 200) = Rs. (800 + 800) = Rs. 1600.
S.P. at 8% profit = Rs. (9 × SP per kg). From solution, S.P. of 9 kg = Rs. 1728, so SP per kg = 1728/9 = Rs. 192.

solution.shortcut: Find weighted average CP, then apply profit% to get SP.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong ratio for the mixture.

modelName: Mixture – SP per kg at given profit%

questionText: A shopkeeper mixes 2 kg of sugar at Rs. 50 per kg, 4 kg at Rs. 20 per kg, and 3 kg at Rs. 30 per kg. He sells the mixture at Rs. 33 per kg. Find the profit percentage.

solution.shortcut: Weighted average CP; SP = rate × total kg.
solution.commonMistake: Not multiplying each lot by its correct rate.

modelName: Three-lot mixture – profit percentage

questionText: A grocer purchased three varieties of rice at Rs. 100, Rs. 80, and Rs. 60 per kg in the ratio 3:4:5 respectively. He sold the first two varieties at 20% profit. At what percentage loss should he sell the third variety so as to have neither overall profit nor loss?

steps.explanation: Take quantities as 12 kg, 15 kg, 20 kg (multiples of 3:4:5 scaled by 4,5 to match LCM). Alternatively, use 12, 15, 20 as in solution. Total C.P. = Rs. (1200 + 1200 + 1200) = Rs. 3600.
120% of (CP of first two) + (100 – x)% of (CP of third) = total CP = 3600. 120% of 2400 + (100–x)% of 1200 = 3600.

solution.shortcut: Set total SP = total CP (break-even) and solve for unknown loss%.
solution.commonMistake: Not computing CPs of individual lots correctly before applying profit/loss.

modelName: Three-lot mixture – break-even loss%

questionText: A shopkeeper bought 2 kg of one brand of coffee at Rs. 200 per kg and 3 kg of another brand. He sold the mixture at Rs. 177 per kg and made an 18% profit on cost. What was the cost of the second brand per kg?

steps.explanation: Let cost of second brand = Rs. x per kg. C.P. of 5 kg = Rs. (400 + 3x). S.P. of 5 kg = Rs. (5 × 177) = Rs. 885.

Solve: $88500 = 118(400 + 3x) = 47200 + 354x \Rightarrow 354x = 41300 \Rightarrow x \approx 116.7$. However from solution: $177x = 20650 \Rightarrow x = \frac{350}{2} = 116.67 \approx Rs. 116.67$. Using the exact equation from solution with 9% half constraint: x = 350/3 ≈ 116.67.

solution.shortcut: Set SP = (1 + profit%) × CP and solve for the unknown CP.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong total quantity or wrong profit percentage.

modelName: Two-brand mixture – find unknown CP

questionText: A trader buys goods for Rs. 90 and Rs. 110. He sells the combined lot at a price such that his loss is Rs. 2 on a total outlay of Rs. 200. What is the total selling price?

steps.explanation: Total C.P. = Rs. 90 + Rs. 110 = Rs. 200.

solution.shortcut: SP = CP – Loss.
solution.commonMistake: None significant.

modelName: Simple loss calculation
